article_title: China, Brazil, Italy and Belgium Join Nuclear Tripling Pledge at Paris Summit as 38 Nations Now Back 2050 Target
summary: Multiple claims not supported by cited sources: Brazil not listed in the declaration text, several unsourced statistics, quotes, and country-specific claims.

content_preview.title: China, Brazil, Italy and Belgium Join Nuclear Tripling Pledge at Paris Summit as 38 Nations Now Back 2050 Target
content_preview.summary: The second Nuclear Energy Summit in Paris brought four new signatories to the Declaration to Triple Nuclear Energy, with the EU announcing a 200 million euro guarantee for advanced reactor investment.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

The second Nuclear Energy Summit, hosted by France and the International Atomic Energy Agency in Paris on March 10, concluded with four additional countries — China, Brazil, Italy and Belgium — endorsing the [Declaration to Triple Nuclear Energy](https://www.elysee.fr/en/emmanuel-macron/2026/03/10/safe-and-affordable-nuclear-energy-for-all) by 2050. The coalition now stands at 38 nations, up from the original 25 that signed at COP28 in Dubai in December 2023.

Representatives from m...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News article at 682 words (within 400-1200 range). Good use of Overview, What We Know, What We Don't Know, and Analysis sections. Appropriate technical depth on nuclear energy policy.
editor_notes.source_verification: Source 1 (IAEA): Accessible. Confirmed: 413 reactors, 377 GW, 31 countries, 69 under construction in 16 countries, ~10% of global electricity, ~25% of low-carbon power, 'nearly 40 countries' pledge, China 4th-gen reactor and Linglong One, EU 200M euro guarantee and SMR strategy, DiNanno quote. NOT confirmed: 140 companies or Google/Amazon/Meta mentions, Germany's position, US 'quadrupling' pledge. Source 2 (Elysée declaration): Accessible. Lists 34 signatory countries. CRITICAL: Brazil is NOT among the 34 listed signatories (China, Belgium, and Italy ARE listed). Confirmed: nuclear applications beyond electricity (industrial heating, AI data centers, hydrogen, desalination, agriculture), deep geological disposal, independent regulatory authorities. NOT confirmed: COP28 '25 original signatories' figure, von der Leyen quote. Source 3 (Diplomatie.gouv.fr): Accessible but provides only general summit program information. Confirms ~10% of global electricity. No specific claims about signatories, commitments, or quotes.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: CRITICAL: Brazil is listed as a new signatory in the title and opening paragraph, but the Elysée declaration (cited as the source for the endorsement) lists 34 countries and Brazil is NOT among them. This is the article's most prominent claim and it is not supported by the cited source. SECONDARY: Several claims lack support from any cited source: (1) 'Over 140 nuclear companies and major energy consumers including Google, Amazon and Meta' — not in any source; (2) Von der Leyen's 'strategic mistake' quote — not in any source; (3) US pledge to 'quadruple' nuclear capacity — IAEA mentions DiNanno but not a quadrupling pledge; (4) Germany's environment minister arguing against nuclear — not in any source; (5) Brazil's 90% clean electricity, uranium reserves, and Amazon SMR proposals — not in any source; (6) '25 original signatories at COP28' — not confirmed by any source (though this is widely known background).
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. Good balance between nuclear ambition and practical caveats in the What We Don't Know and Analysis sections.
editor_notes.originality: No duplicate articles found on this topic in recent published content.
concerns: CRITICAL: Brazil not listed among the 34 signatories in the Elysée declaration — article's title and lead claim it as a new signatory

Multiple unsourced claims: 140 companies with Google/Amazon/Meta, von der Leyen 'strategic mistake' quote, US quadrupling pledge, Germany's environment minister position, Brazil's energy profile details
The article makes at least 6 specific claims that cannot be traced to any of the three cited sources, violating the editorial policy that every claim needs a source
recommendations: Verify Brazil's status — if Brazil endorsed separately from the formal declaration, add a source that confirms this and clarify the distinction
Add sources for unsourced claims (von der Leyen quote, US quadrupling pledge, 140 companies, Germany's position) or remove them
Consider adding a Reuters/AP wire source that covers the summit more broadly to support the claims not found in the institutional sources
editor_notes.overall_assessment: The article is well-written and covers an important energy policy event, but has significant source-verification gaps. The most critical issue is that Brazil — featured prominently in the title — is not listed as a signatory in the declaration text the article cites. Multiple other claims (von der Leyen quote, US quadrupling pledge, 140 companies, Germany's position) cannot be traced to any cited source. REQUEST_CHANGES to address these factual and sourcing gaps.
